persona: Someone seeking advice on dealing with a passive-aggressive coworker

turns.content: Ugh, I don't know how much more of this I can take. I've been working with Karen for like 3 years and it's always something. She's always 'forgetting' to do her part of a project, but somehow it gets done and she looks good. I feel like I'm doing all the heavy lifting around here and it's just so frustrating.

I can sense your frustration. It sounds like you're feeling undervalued and overworked. Can you tell me more about the specific behaviors or incidents that are bothering you the most? Was there a recent situation that triggered this feeling?

Honestly, it's just the constant 'accidental' delays and 'miscommunications' that drive me crazy. Like, one time we had a big presentation coming up and Karen 'forgot' to send me the crucial data, so I had to redo the whole thing at the last minute. And when I called her out on it, she just gave me this fake smile and said 'oops' like it was no big deal. It's just so infuriating.

I can imagine how infuriating that must have been. It sounds like Karen might be using passive-aggressive behavior to avoid taking responsibility for her actions. Have you tried talking to her directly about how her behavior is affecting you, or is there a particular reason you're hesitant to do so?

Yeah, I've tried talking to her, but it just ends up in a weird, awkward silence or she'll just say something like 'oh, sorry, I didn't mean to do that' and then just go back to ignoring my requests. I don't know what to do anymore. I feel like I'm just enabling her by being too nice.

It's possible that Karen is using guilt or emotional manipulation to get you to back down. In that case, it might be helpful to set clear boundaries and expectations with her. For example, you could say something like 'I need you to send me the data by Friday so I can finalize the presentation. Can I trust that you'll get it to me by then?' By being direct and specific, you can help prevent misunderstandings and set a clear track for your collaboration.
